pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Women changing the world

pageCaption.value: Strong, resilient women are leading the way to a better future with support from the Rolex Awards for Enterprise.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex Awards for Enterprise 2021 - Women changing the world

metadescription.value: Five female Rolex Awards Laureates share how their life journeys led them to tackle some of the world’s most intractable problems. More on Rolex.org

previewTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Women changing the world

text.value:
Strong, resilient women are leading the way to a better future with support from the Rolex Awards for Enterprise.

We asked five women Laureates, all pioneers, to tell us how their life’s journey led them to tackle some of the world’s most intractable problems, from recycling formerly unrecyclable plastics to desertification in Africa. Through their Award-winning projects, they demonstrate that a tenacious will and courage laced with intelligence can overcome the greatest challenges.

I work in Austria, but I grew up in the United Kingdom where I discovered from an early age that I adored exploring underground. On the advice of a lecturer to “do what you enjoy”, I found a way to study cave science at university and turn it into a career.

Caves provide one of the last frontiers on Earth for true exploration and the planet’s most northerly caves in north Greenland are so difficult to access that no one has been able to look inside, even though we have known about them since the 1960s. That will change in 2023 with a world-first expedition I am leading, thanks to the Rolex Awards. They are pretty much the only programme that could or would support an exploratory initiative like this one.

Caves are like time capsules. We know that Greenland used to be warmer and wetter. We hope to find mineral deposits that could extend the existing climate record for the far north fourfold – up to half a million years ago. The Arctic is currently warming at more than twice the global average. Understanding more about how this sensitive part of the world responds to a warmer world is crucial. Along with sea-levels rise, increasing temperatures in Greenland will influence global patterns of rainfall, ice formations, ocean currents and weather systems affecting heavily populated areas around the world. My dream is that our findings will make their way into the climate modelling used to create environmental policies.

title.value: The Arctic is currently warming at more than twice the global average.

text.value:
I grew up in the United Kingdom, which, like the rest of Europe, has no tropical coral reefs. My first sighting of these spectacular ecosystems came at the age of around seven when my father took me on a snorkelling holiday to the tropics. I was just blown away. That was when my love for reefs really began.

Now, over 25 years later, the fascination is as strong as ever. But they are severely threatened. Many scientists fear that the world’s coral reefs, at least as we know them, could be lost by 2050, victims of human activity and climate change. Hopefully they will be proved wrong. I just don’t want to be part of the generation that says: “We lost the coral reefs.” Corals are not just intricate and beautiful – they also support hundreds of millions of human lives through the ecosystems services they provide.

My research focuses on hot spots of coral resilience on Australia’s Great Barrier Reef, trying to identify why some corals survive environmental stress, and testing how they respond when transplanted to areas devastated by mass coral death. My job perfectly matches my lifelong passion – Deputy Team Leader of the Future Reefs Program at the University of Technology Sydney. I also have research grants and have been awarded numerous honours within Australia and beyond. Winning a Rolex Award has been a huge help to my research and to my ability to communicate my research in a way that will engage people to help save our reefs. I also try to promote women and girls studying the STEM subjects – Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics.

linkName.value: Reef rescue with the toughest corals

title.value: Many scientists fear that the world’s coral reefs, at least as we know them, could be lost by 2050, victims of human activity and climate change.

text.value:
I was born in China and moved to Canada with my family at age five. My campaign to solve one of today’s great challenges – the pollution caused by plastic waste – began in my teens, after visiting a waste-management plant in Vancouver with my best friend Jeanny Yao. Since then, we have collaborated to face up to this problem.

We’ve got lots of recognition for our campaign – we did a TED talk at age 19, in 2013, about treating plastic with technology. But it’s not recognition we’re chasing, it’s solutions. Less than a tenth of the world’s plastic is recycled – and we’re producing more than 300 million tonnes a year. Plastic doesn’t break down for hundreds to thousands of years, and production is expected to double globally in the next 15 years.

After a number of years exploring different ways to break down and make use of plastic waste, Jeanny and I raised over US$12 million in capital between 2015–2021 to set up Novoloop (formerly named BioCellection) in Silicon Valley to pioneer solutions to the plastics crisis. Having Rolex as a supporter and being able to share our message through the Rolex Awards is having a massive impact. We are focused on the worst of all the plastics, polyethylene, used for plastic bags. We’re the first to invent a process that can turn it into valuable performance materials used, for example, to manufacture the soles of running shoes. Our technology can achieve this with a 46 per cent CO2e reduction when compared to best practices in the industry. We must act quickly. If humans want to continue living on this planet, we must solve the plastic problem. At the current rate, we will have more plastic than fish in our oceans by 2050.

title.value: Plastic doesn’t break down for hundreds to thousands of years, and production is expected to double globally in the next 15 years.

text.value:
I trained as a radiologist. But there was a time in my life, after I had my first daughter and moved to a new city, that I had to give it up. I became severely depressed. The only thing that saved me was to find a way to conduct consultations from home via a video platform. That gave me the idea for Sehat Kehani, which I co-founded with a colleague in 2017.

We are addressing two interconnected problems in Pakistan. Firstly, we have 200,000 doctors to serve a population of 200 million, half of whom never see a qualified doctor in their lives. Secondly, 63 per cent of medical students are women, with more than half dropping out of medicine when they marry and have children. It's called the Doctor Bride phenomenon.

Through Sehat Kehani, we offer women doctors the opportunity to work from home and connect them to patients who live in underserved and remote areas. Through our application, people can contact a doctor 24/7, but in places where people don’t have access to the internet, we upgrade traditional clinics into telemedicine centres where people can go to consult a doctor online. We are giving more than three million people access to affordable, quality healthcare through a network of thousands of doctors.

I’m an idealist. I believe that healthcare change will come in Pakistan and that digital healthcare will transform it. Things are changing. It might be slow, it might be difficult, but I’m very hopeful.

linkName.value: Women doctors for telemedicine in Pakistan

title.value: We are giving more than three million people access to affordable, quality healthcare through a network of thousands of doctors.

text.value:
When I came to my father’s homeland from France in 1996, Tunisia was green. With climate change, however, rainfall dropped by 70 per cent and the desert steadily encroached on fertile lands, forcing people to leave as their crops were invaded by sand.

I could see the poor conditions that surrounded the country and, especially, the social and economic impact on my family. In 2012, I moved to Tunisia, and created Acacias for All, which developed into 1milliontrees4Tunisia, helping to train farmers in soil restoration techniques using the tough acacia tree to fight desertification. More than 700,000 trees have now been planted.

I also founded the NGO Dream in Tunisia to help women develop their potential and Plug&Green, a digital platform connecting African farmers with international companies. For several years, I had the honour of serving on Emmanuel Macron’s Presidential Council for Africa and have now joined the Great Green Wall Accelerator as a programme officer. It’s a programme run by the United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification to restore 100 million hectares of degraded landscapes in 11 countries of the Sahel by 2030. These restored lands have the potential to create jobs, sequestrate carbon and improve the lives of the 300 million people of the Sahel. Sustainability, stability and security. Pragmatism will help conquer the world’s ecological challenges. It makes me optimistic for the future.

title.value: These restored lands have the potential to create jobs, sequestrate carbon and improve the lives of the 300 million people of the Sahel.
